title: NoSQL Operator Injection via Unsanitized User Input
description: User-controlled input is passed directly into a MongoDB query without sanitization, allowing attackers to inject NoSQL operators such as $gt, $lt, $ne, $regex, or $where to bypass authentication, exfiltrate data, or manipulate query logic.

remediation.description: Use input validation and sanitization libraries such as express-mongo-sanitize or mongo-sanitize. Validate expected types before passing user input to queries. Never pass raw request bodies directly into query objects.

title: SQL Injection via String Concatenation or Template Literals
description: SQL queries are constructed by concatenating or interpolating user-controlled input directly into query strings, allowing attackers to inject arbitrary SQL commands to read, modify, or delete data, or escalate privileges.

remediation.description: Always use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Never concatenate or interpolate user input into SQL strings. Use an ORM or query builder that handles escaping automatically.

title: Database Connection Using Default or Hardcoded Credentials
description: Database connection strings contain default, well-known, or hardcoded credentials. Attackers can use these credentials to gain full access to the database if the service is exposed or the source code is leaked.

remediation.description: Store database credentials in environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, etc.). Never hardcode credentials in source code. Use strong, unique passwords and rotate them regularly.

title: Database Authentication Mechanism Disabled
description: The database server is configured to run without authentication, allowing any client with network access to connect and perform operations without providing credentials.

remediation.description: Always enable authentication on database servers. Configure role-based access control (RBAC) and enforce the principle of least privilege. Use SCRAM-SHA-256 or x.509 certificate authentication for MongoDB, and md5/scram-sha-256 for PostgreSQL.
remediation.code_example:
# MongoDB mongod.conf
security:
  authorization: enabled

# PostgreSQL pg_hba.conf
host all all 0.0.0.0/0 scram-sha-256

# Redis redis.conf
requirepass your_strong_password_here
protected-mode yes

title: Database Encryption at Rest or in Transit Disabled
description: The database is configured with encryption disabled, leaving data vulnerable to interception during transit or unauthorized access on disk. This includes disabled TLS/SSL connections and disabled storage encryption.

remediation.description: Enable TLS/SSL for all database connections. Enable encryption at rest using the database's built-in encryption or filesystem-level encryption. Use strong cipher suites and valid certificates.

title: Overly Permissive Database Grants and Privileges
description: Database permissions are configured with excessive privileges such as GRANT ALL ON *.* or WITH GRANT OPTION, violating the principle of least privilege. Compromised accounts with these permissions can read, modify, or destroy all data across all databases.

remediation.description: Follow the principle of least privilege. Grant only the specific permissions needed for each application role. Avoid GRANT ALL and WITH GRANT OPTION. Use separate database accounts for different services and operations (read-only, read-write, admin).

title: Database Exposed on All Network Interfaces
description: The database is configured to listen on 0.0.0.0 or all interfaces, making it accessible from any network including the public internet. Combined with weak authentication, this allows remote attackers to connect directly to the database.

remediation.description: Bind database services to specific internal interfaces (127.0.0.1 or private network IPs). Use firewall rules to restrict access to authorized hosts only. Place databases in private subnets with no direct internet access. Use SSH tunnels or VPN for remote administration.
remediation.code_example:
# MongoDB mongod.conf — bind to localhost only
net:
  bindIp: 127.0.0.1

# PostgreSQL postgresql.conf
listen_addresses = '127.0.0.1'

# Redis redis.conf
bind 127.0.0.1 ::1

title: Unencrypted Database Backup Operations
description: Database backup commands are executed without encryption flags, producing unencrypted dump files that may contain sensitive data. If these backups are stored on shared storage, transmitted over unencrypted channels, or left on compromised systems, the data is fully exposed.

remediation.description: Encrypt all database backups using GPG, OpenSSL, or cloud-native encryption (AWS KMS, GCP CMEK). Use TLS for backup transfers. Store backups in encrypted storage with restricted access. Implement backup retention policies and verify backup integrity regularly.
remediation.code_example:
# Encrypted MongoDB backup
mongodump --uri="$MONGO_URI" --tls --archive | gpg --encrypt --recipient backup@company.com > backup.archive.gpg

# Encrypted PostgreSQL backup
pg_dump --format=custom "$DATABASE_URL" | gpg --symmetric --cipher-algo AES256 > backup.dump.gpg

# Encrypted MySQL backup
mysqldump -u "$DB_USER" -p"$DB_PASS" --all-databases | openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -out backup.sql.enc
